payload.title: Import Content Completeness
payload.statement: The ddis import command must emit synthetic events for ALL content types: modules, invariants, ADRs, sections, cross-references, negative specs, quality gates, glossary entries.
payload.semi_formal: forall T in ContentTypes: count(import_events(T)) = count(parsed_db(T))
payload.violation_scenario: Import emits invariant and ADR events but skips sections, cross-references, and negative specs. Materialize produces database missing sections. project renders incomplete modules.
payload.validation_method: Parse CLI spec, import, materialize, compare row counts per table against parsed database.
payload.why_this_matters: Without content completeness, the import-materialize pipeline cannot produce equivalent output to parse, breaking the self-bootstrap guarantee.

payload.title: Applier Spec-ID Parameterization
payload.statement: The sqlApplier derives spec_id from event payloads or configuration, never hardcoding values. Every inserted row references the correct spec_index and source_files entries.
payload.semi_formal: forall row in applier_output: row.spec_id = config.spec_id AND exists(spec_index[row.spec_id])
payload.violation_scenario: sqlApplier hardcodes spec_id=1, source_file_id=1. When materializing events from a spec whose index row is ID 3, all rows reference non-existent spec_id=1.
payload.validation_method: Materialize events into fresh database, verify spec_id references resolve via foreign key check.
payload.why_this_matters: Hardcoded IDs break multi-spec databases and prevent the applier from being reusable across different specs.

payload.title: Projector Section Rendering
payload.statement: The project command filters invariants, ADRs, sections, and other content by module ownership. Each projected module contains only the elements maintained by that module.
payload.semi_formal: project(S,M).invariants = S.invariants WHERE module=M
payload.violation_scenario: project queries all invariants for every module. Module A receives invariants owned by Module B, producing incorrect specification documents.
payload.validation_method: Project multi-module spec, verify each output file contains only elements from its maintains list.
payload.why_this_matters: Without per-module filtering, projections conflate module boundaries and produce incorrect specification documents.

payload.title: Single Write Path
payload.statement: Content-mutating commands (crystallize, refine, absorb, witness, challenge) write ONLY to the event log. SQLite databases are produced exclusively by materialize; markdown files are produced exclusively by project.
payload.semi_formal: forall cmd in {crystallize, refine, absorb, witness, challenge}: writes(cmd) subset_of EventLog
payload.violation_scenario: Crystallize writes directly to both the event log AND the markdown file. A crash between the two writes produces inconsistent state.
payload.validation_method: Instrument write calls in crystallize, verify only event stream writes occur. Materialize + project reconstruct all state.
payload.why_this_matters: Dual-write paths create consistency hazards. Event log as single write target enables reliable replay and crash recovery.

payload.title: Deprecation Compatibility Bridge
payload.statement: During migration Phase B, deprecated commands (parse, render) produce structurally equivalent output to their replacements (import+materialize, project). Both emit deprecation warnings.
payload.semi_formal: output(deprecated_cmd(x)) ~ output(replacement(x)) AND stderr contains deprecation warning
payload.violation_scenario: parse command is replaced by import+materialize but produces different section ordering, breaking existing workflows that depend on parse output format.
payload.validation_method: Run parse and import+materialize on same input, compare via StructuralDiff.
payload.why_this_matters: Migration must not break existing workflows. Structural equivalence ensures backward compatibility during transition.

payload.title: Processor Idempotency
payload.statement: Processor Handle is deterministic: given the same event and state, it produces the same derived events. During re-fold, processors fire only on primary events (those without derived_by in payload), preventing duplicate derived events.
payload.semi_formal: p.Handle(e, state) is deterministic; re-fold deduplicates via skip-derived-events
payload.violation_scenario: Re-fold fires processors on both primary and derived events, producing duplicate validation warnings as new derived events.
payload.validation_method: Fold events twice, verify derived event count is identical. Verify processors skip events with derived_by field.
payload.why_this_matters: Without idempotency, re-fold produces ever-growing derived event streams, making replay non-deterministic.

payload.title: Processor Failure Isolation
payload.statement: A processor error is non-fatal. The error is logged, derived events from the failed processor are discarded, and fold continues with the next event. No processor failure can halt materialization.
payload.semi_formal: forall p in processors: error(p.Handle(e)) => log(error) AND discard(derived(p,e)) AND continue(fold)
payload.violation_scenario: Validation processor panics on malformed invariant. Fold aborts, leaving database in partial state with no recovery path.
payload.validation_method: Register processor that returns error, verify fold completes and result contains error log but all primary events applied.
payload.why_this_matters: Processor isolation ensures materialization reliability. A buggy processor cannot corrupt the materialized state.

payload.title: Derived Event Provenance
payload.statement: Derived events carry the triggering event ID in their causes array and a derived_by field in their payload identifying the processor that produced them.
payload.semi_formal: forall d in derived_events: d.causes contains trigger_event_id AND d.payload.derived_by = processor_name
payload.violation_scenario: Processor emits derived validation event without causes reference. During bisect, the derived event cannot be traced to its trigger, breaking causal provenance.
payload.validation_method: Register processor, fold events, verify all derived events have causes and derived_by fields populated.
payload.why_this_matters: Without provenance, derived events are opaque. Causal tracing and bisect require knowing which primary event triggered each derived event.

payload.title: Snapshot Creation Determinism
payload.statement: StateHash computed over canonicalized content tables is deterministic. Two materializations of the same event prefix produce identical state hashes.
payload.semi_formal: StateHash(materialize(L[0:p])) = StateHash(materialize(L[0:p])) always
payload.violation_scenario: StateHash includes auto-increment IDs or timestamps. Two materializations of identical events produce different hashes, making snapshot verification fail.
payload.validation_method: Materialize same events twice into separate databases, compute StateHash for each, verify equality.
payload.why_this_matters: Deterministic hashing enables snapshot verification. If hashes are non-deterministic, snapshots cannot be trusted.

payload.title: Snapshot Monotonicity
payload.statement: Snapshots are monotonically ordered by position. A snapshot created later has a position greater than or equal to any earlier snapshot.
payload.semi_formal: s1.created_at < s2.created_at => s1.position <= s2.position
payload.violation_scenario: Snapshot created at position 500 is followed by snapshot at position 300, causing accelerated fold to skip events between 300-500.
payload.validation_method: Create multiple snapshots during fold, verify positions are monotonically non-decreasing.
payload.why_this_matters: Non-monotonic snapshots break accelerated fold by creating gaps in event coverage.

payload.title: Snapshot Recovery Graceful Degradation
payload.statement: If a snapshot is corrupted or its StateHash does not match the materialized state, the system falls back to full replay from the beginning. No silent corruption occurs.
payload.semi_formal: verify(snapshot) = false => full_replay(log[0:]); never silent_use(corrupted_snapshot)
payload.violation_scenario: Corrupted snapshot passes verification. Fold resumes from corrupt state, producing incorrect database without any error indication.
payload.validation_method: Corrupt snapshot file, attempt accelerated fold, verify system detects corruption and falls back to full replay.
payload.why_this_matters: Silent snapshot corruption would produce incorrect state with no warning. Graceful degradation ensures correctness even with disk errors.

payload.title: Pipeline Round-Trip Preservation
payload.statement: The full event-sourcing pipeline (parse to import to materialize to project to parse) preserves all structural content. The round-trip output is structurally equivalent to the input modulo metadata.
payload.semi_formal: StructuralDiff(parse(project(materialize(import(parse(spec))))), parse(spec)) = empty
payload.violation_scenario: Project drops glossary entries during rendering. Re-parsing the projected output produces a database missing glossary terms.
payload.validation_method: Full round-trip on CLI spec, compare parsed databases via StructuralDiff.
payload.why_this_matters: Round-trip preservation is the ultimate correctness guarantee for the event-sourcing pipeline.

payload.title: E2E Pipeline Determinism
payload.statement: Running the full event-sourcing pipeline twice on the same input produces byte-identical output. The pipeline is a pure function with no hidden state.
payload.semi_formal: pipeline(spec) = pipeline(spec) always (byte-identical)
payload.violation_scenario: Timestamps embedded in JSONL events differ between runs. Second materialize produces different event IDs, breaking determinism.
payload.validation_method: Run pipeline twice on CLI spec, compare output files byte-for-byte.
payload.why_this_matters: Non-deterministic pipelines prevent reproducible builds and make testing unreliable.

payload.title: Self-Bootstrap Pipeline as Integration Gate
payload.problem: The event-sourcing pipeline (import, materialize, project) must be verified against the CLI spec itself. Without self-bootstrap testing, pipeline bugs go undetected until users encounter them.
payload.options: A) Manual testing against sample specs. B) Unit tests with synthetic fixtures. C) Self-bootstrap: run the pipeline on the CLI spec itself as a CI gate.
payload.decision: Option C: Self-bootstrap. The CLI spec is the most comprehensive test fixture available. Running the full pipeline on it exercises all content types, cross-references, and edge cases.
payload.consequences: Self-bootstrap is the gold standard for compiler-like tools. It catches real-world bugs that synthetic fixtures miss. The CLI spec exercises all 10 domains, 97 invariants, and 74 ADRs.
payload.tests: TestSelfBootstrapEventPipeline in tests/pipeline_integration_test.go: parse CLI spec, import, materialize, compare via StructuralDiff.

payload.title: Structural Equivalence Definition
payload.problem: APP-INV-078 requires import+materialize to equal parse, but what does equality mean? Row IDs, timestamps, and raw_text differ by design.
payload.options: A) Byte-identical comparison. B) Row-count comparison per table. C) Content-only comparison excluding metadata fields (auto-increment id, parsed_at, created_at, raw_text, content_hash).
payload.decision: Option C: Content-only structural comparison. Compare invariant_id, title, statement, semi_formal, section_path, term, definition, etc. Exclude auto-increment id, parsed_at, created_at, raw_text, content_hash. Implemented as StructuralDiff function.
payload.consequences: Byte-identical is too strict (timestamps differ). Row-count is too loose (content could be wrong). Content-only comparison tests what matters: the structured information.

payload.title: Phased Migration Strategy
payload.problem: Transitioning from direct markdown/SQLite writes to event-only writes cannot be done atomically. Existing commands and tests depend on the current write paths.
payload.options: A) Big-bang migration: rewrite all commands at once. B) Phased migration: Phase A (bridge), Phase B (event-first with wrappers), Phase C (remove old paths). C) Feature flag per command.
payload.decision: Option B: Phased migration. Phase A (done): import bridge, events emitted alongside SQL. Phase B: event-first with deprecation wrappers for parse and render. Phase C: remove old write paths entirely.
payload.consequences: Phased migration allows incremental verification at each step. Structural equivalence (APP-INV-089) gates each phase transition.
payload.tests: TestParseDeprecationWrapper, TestRenderDeprecationWrapper verify Phase B wrappers produce equivalent output.

payload.title: Crystallize Event-Only Path
payload.problem: Crystallize currently writes to both the event log AND the markdown file. This dual-write violates APP-INV-088 (Single Write Path).
payload.options: A) Keep dual-write for reliability. B) Event-only with separate project step. C) Event-only with automatic internal project (auto-project).
payload.decision: Option C: Crystallize emits event, then optionally runs materialize+project internally. Users see markdown update immediately but it came from the event, not a direct write. Use --no-project to skip auto-projection.
payload.consequences: Auto-project preserves the UX of immediate markdown updates while enforcing single write path. The event is always the source of truth.
payload.tests: TestCrystallizeEventOnly verifies no direct markdown write occurs. TestCrystallizeAutoProject verifies markdown is produced via materialize+project.

payload.title: Processor Registration Mechanism
payload.problem: Stream processors need a registration mechanism that supports built-in processors and extensibility.
payload.options: A) Hardcoded processor list. B) Plugin system with dynamic loading. C) Registry with RegisterProcessor() API and 3 built-in processors.
payload.decision: Option C: Engine provides RegisterProcessor(name, Processor) API. Three built-in processors (validation, consistency, drift) are registered by default in CLI. Extension via RegisterProcessor() for custom processors.
payload.consequences: RegisterProcessor API balances simplicity with extensibility. Built-in processors handle the common cases. Custom processors enable domain-specific reactive logic without modifying the engine.

payload.title: Derived Event Deduplication
payload.problem: During re-fold, processors fire on every event including previously-derived events, creating duplicate derived events.
payload.options: A) Track processed event IDs in engine state. B) Skip-derived-events: processors fire only on primary events (those without derived_by in payload). C) Idempotent processors that detect and skip duplicates.
payload.decision: Option B: Skip-derived-events strategy. During fold, events with derived_by in their payload are applied to the database (they carry valid mutations) but processors are NOT re-fired on them. This prevents duplicate derived events during re-fold.
payload.consequences: Skip-derived-events is the simplest correct strategy. It requires no state tracking and no processor-side deduplication logic. The distinction between primary and derived events is already in the payload.
payload.tests: TestSkipDerivedEvents verifies processors fire only on primary events. TestReFoldIdempotency verifies re-fold produces same derived event count.

payload.title: Snapshot as SQLite State Hash
payload.problem: Snapshots need a verification mechanism to detect corruption and ensure consistency.
payload.options: A) File checksum (SHA-256 of entire SQLite file). B) State hash: SHA-256 over canonicalized content tables excluding auto-IDs and timestamps. C) Row-count comparison.
payload.decision: Option B: State hash over canonicalized content tables. SHA-256 is computed over deterministic serialization of all content tables (sorted rows, content fields only, excluding auto-increment IDs, timestamps, raw_text). Same function used for both snapshot verification and structural equivalence comparison.
payload.consequences: File checksum is too brittle (SQLite internal layout varies). Row-count is too loose. State hash tests exactly what matters: structural content identity.

payload.title: Automatic Snapshot Interval
payload.problem: Snapshots must be created at appropriate intervals during materialization to enable accelerated fold.
payload.options: A) Manual snapshot creation only. B) Time-based interval (every N seconds). C) Event-count-based interval (every N events), configurable via --snapshot-interval, 0 to disable.
payload.decision: Option C: Event-count-based interval. Default every 1000 events, configurable via --snapshot-interval flag on materialize command. Setting to 0 disables automatic snapshots. Manual snapshot creation always available via snapshot create subcommand.
payload.consequences: Event-count-based interval is predictable and independent of wall-clock time. The 1000-event default balances snapshot overhead against fold acceleration for typical spec sizes.

payload.title: E2E Test Architecture
payload.problem: Event-sourcing tests span multiple packages and commands. Need to decide on test architecture for integration and E2E tests.
payload.options: A) Pure unit tests with mocks at every boundary. B) In-process integration tests using Go test framework. C) Hybrid: in-process for behavioral tests, subprocess for E2E pipeline tests. Real CLI spec as fixture.
payload.decision: Option C: Hybrid test architecture. Behavioral tests (APP-INV-085..097) run in-process using Go testing framework with real database operations. E2E pipeline test runs subprocess commands (ddis parse, import, materialize, project) on the actual CLI spec as fixture.
payload.consequences: In-process tests are fast and debuggable. Subprocess E2E tests catch integration issues (flag parsing, file I/O, error handling) that in-process tests miss. The CLI spec is the canonical fixture.
payload.tests: tests/invariant_behavioral_test.go for behavioral tests. tests/e2e_pipeline_test.go for E2E pipeline test. tests/pipeline_integration_test.go for self-bootstrap integration test.

payload.title: Snapshot Position Event-Stream Ordinal
payload.statement: The snapshot position field MUST represent the count of events processed from the canonical event stream, NOT a count of materialized content elements (e.g., invariants, sections). Position forms a monotone counter in the stream's ordinal space: it strictly increases with each applied event and enables FoldFrom() to resume at the correct stream offset.
payload.semi_formal: Let pos(s) be the position stored in snapshot s, and let |E_applied| be the number of events applied during the fold that produced s. Then pos(s) = |E_applied|. For any subsequent snapshot s' with pos(s') > pos(s), FoldFrom(events, pos(s)) must apply exactly the events at indices [pos(s), pos(s')-1].
payload.violation_scenario: Snapshot created with position = COUNT(invariants). After an invariant upsert (no new row), position stays at N. After an invariant deletion, position decreases to N-1. FoldFrom(events, N) skips or replays wrong events, producing divergent state.
payload.validation_method: Create snapshot, count events in stream file, verify snapshot.Position == event count. Delete an invariant from DB, create new snapshot, verify position still equals event count (not invariant count).
payload.why_this_matters: Snapshot-accelerated fold (APP-INV-094) relies on position to skip already-applied events. If position tracks a projection (invariant count) instead of the stream ordinal, the skip window is wrong, breaking idempotency (APP-INV-075) and determinism (APP-INV-097).

payload.title: Manifest Mutation via Structured Serialization
payload.statement: Any programmatic modification to manifest.yaml MUST use a YAML parser for reading and a YAML serializer for writing. String concatenation, substring insertion, or regex-based edits on the raw file content are prohibited. The serialization round-trip must preserve all existing keys, comments within the YAML data model, and structural relationships.
payload.semi_formal: Let M be a manifest file, parse(M) its YAML AST, and serialize(AST) the inverse. For any mutation f on the AST: serialize(f(parse(M))) must be valid YAML AND parse(serialize(f(parse(M)))) = f(parse(M)). String-level operations g(text(M)) do NOT satisfy this because g is not a homomorphism from the character monoid to the YAML CFG.
payload.violation_scenario: crystallize command appends a registry entry via string concat before the last newline. If the file lacks a trailing newline, the entry is appended to the last line. If a concurrent crystallize interleaves, both writes corrupt each other. If the registry section has inline YAML anchors, the appended entry may reference undefined anchors.
payload.validation_method: Round-trip test: parse manifest → mutate AST → serialize → parse again → verify equality. Concurrent test: two parallel crystallize calls → verify both entries present and YAML valid. Edge case: manifest without trailing newline.
payload.why_this_matters: The manifest is the spec's structural skeleton (APP-ADR-010). Corrupting it via string manipulation breaks parse (APP-INV-001), module resolution, and the entire bilateral lifecycle. The crystallize command is the primary spec authoring path (APP-INV-088) — it must be safe.

payload.title: Event Applier Section Hierarchy Preservation
payload.statement: The event fold applier MUST preserve section hierarchy when materializing events into the SQLite state. Content elements (invariants, ADRs, glossary entries, negative specs, quality gates) MUST be associated with their correct containing section via section_id. Hardcoding section_id to a constant (e.g., 0) is prohibited.
payload.semi_formal: Let E be a content event with payload containing section_path P. Let S be the section table after applying all section events. Then Apply(E).section_id = lookup(S, P).id. The function lookup: SectionPath -> SectionID is total over the domain of section paths present in prior section events.
payload.violation_scenario: sqlApplier.InsertInvariant sets section_id=0 for all invariants. Coverage analysis queries invariants by section — returns empty. project command reconstructs modules without section structure. Validation Check 5 cannot verify invariants are in correct sections.
payload.validation_method: After fold: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM invariants WHERE section_id = 0 MUST return 0. For each invariant with a section_path in its event payload, verify section_id points to the correct section row. Run ddis coverage on materialized DB — verify per-section completeness is computable.
payload.why_this_matters: Section hierarchy is the structural backbone of DDIS specs (§0.1 State Space). Without it, the materialized state is a flat bag of elements — losing the tree structure that enables scoped queries, module-section relationships, and structural validation. This breaks the round-trip guarantee (APP-INV-096).

payload.title: Structural Diff Composite Key Completeness
payload.statement: The StructuralDiff function MUST use composite keys that include ALL discriminant fields when building comparison maps. For cross-references, the key MUST include (ref_type, ref_target, ref_text). Omitting any discriminant field from the key can cause silent collision and data loss in the diff output.
payload.semi_formal: For any table T with natural key K = (k1, k2, ..., kn), the diff map key MUST be the full tuple K. For cross_references, K = (ref_type, ref_target, ref_text). The map function m: Row -> Key is injective iff K contains all discriminant columns. If m is not injective, |image(m)| < |domain(m)| and the diff loses rows.
payload.violation_scenario: diffCrossRefs uses key = target|text, omitting ref_type. Two cross-refs (type=invariant, target=APP-INV-071, text=See INV) and (type=app_invariant, target=APP-INV-071, text=See INV) collide. The second overwrites the first in the map. StateHash computed on the diff is wrong.
payload.validation_method: Insert two cross-refs with same target+text but different ref_type into DB1. Insert only one into DB2. Run StructuralDiff. Verify BOTH additions are reported, not just one.
payload.why_this_matters: StructuralDiff is the foundation for StateHash (APP-INV-093), snapshot verification, and the event-sourcing integrity chain. A lossy diff means snapshots can verify as correct when they actually diverge.

payload.title: LLM Confidence Constant Centralization
payload.statement: All LLM-based majority vote confidence calculations MUST use centralized constants. For N-of-M agreement, the confidence value MUST be identical regardless of which module (witness evaluation, consistency checking, or any future LLM tier) computes it. Specifically: unanimous (3/3) = 0.95, majority (2/3) = 0.80.
payload.semi_formal: Let conf: (agreement, total) -> [0,1] be the confidence function. conf is a SINGLE function shared by all modules. For all callers c1, c2: conf_c1(k, n) = conf_c2(k, n). Currently violated: conf_eval(2,3) = 0.75 != conf_llm(2,3) = 0.80.
payload.violation_scenario: Witness eval records a 2/3 agreement witness with confidence 0.75. Consistency checker finds the same pair with 2/3 agreement at confidence 0.80. Deduplication keeps the consistency finding (higher confidence) even though both represent the same statistical evidence.
payload.validation_method: grep -r for hardcoded confidence constants (0.75, 0.80, 0.85, 0.95). Verify all originate from a single source of truth. Test: call both eval and consistency with mock 2/3 agreement — verify identical confidence.
payload.why_this_matters: Confidence values feed into deduplication, prioritization, and report rendering. Inconsistent values create ordering anomalies where identical evidence is ranked differently based on the code path, violating statistical soundness (APP-INV-055).

payload.title: Structured YAML Serialization for Manifest Mutations
payload.problem: The crystallize command modifies manifest.yaml by appending strings before the last newline. This bypasses the YAML grammar, risking corruption on edge cases (no trailing newline, concurrent writes, inline comments) and violating the bilateral lifecycle's requirement that all spec authoring be mechanically sound.
payload.options:
Option A: YAML parse-mutate-serialize via gopkg.in/yaml.v3 (already a dependency). Reads file, unmarshals to map, modifies map, marshals back. Preserves structure.
Option B: Template-based generation. Regenerate entire manifest from DB state each time. Loss of manual formatting.
Option C: Patch-based editing via a YAML-aware diff/patch library. More complex, handles concurrent edits.
Option D: Status quo (string concatenation). Rejected due to corruption risk.
payload.decision: Option A: Use gopkg.in/yaml.v3 Unmarshal/Marshal for all manifest mutations. Read file, unmarshal to typed struct, modify struct fields, marshal back. This is the simplest correct approach and uses an existing dependency.
payload.consequences: yaml.v3 is already in go.mod. The manifest structure is well-defined (ManifestData struct in parser/manifest.go). Unmarshal → mutate → Marshal is a standard pattern that preserves YAML validity by construction. Template-based (B) loses manual formatting. Patch-based (C) is over-engineered for the current use case. String concat (D) is provably unsafe.
payload.tests:
1. Round-trip: parse manifest → add registry entry → serialize → parse again → verify entry present and all existing entries preserved.
2. Concurrent: two parallel crystallize calls → both entries in final YAML.
3. Edge cases: no trailing newline, empty registry, duplicate entry (idempotent).
4. Format preservation: existing entries not reordered or reformatted.

payload.title: Event Schema Carries Section Path for Hierarchy Reconstruction
payload.problem: The event fold applier (sqlApplier) hardcodes section_id=0 for all content elements because events do not carry section path information. This destroys the section hierarchy that is fundamental to DDIS spec structure, making coverage analysis, section-scoped queries, and structural validation impossible on materialized state.
payload.options:
Option A: Add section_path to content event payloads. During fold, look up or create the section row and use its ID. Events become self-contained.
Option B: Run a post-fold reconciliation pass that matches elements to sections by line number or name heuristics. Fragile and lossy.
Option C: Store section events separately and reconstruct the tree before applying content events. Requires event ordering guarantees.
Option D: Accept section_id=0 and disable section-dependent features for materialized state. Violates round-trip (APP-INV-096).
payload.decision: Option A: Enrich content event payloads with a section_path field. The applier looks up the section by path (creating it if needed via a synthetic section event). This is the correct approach because it makes events self-describing and enables correct fold without external state.
payload.consequences: Events are the source of truth (APP-INV-071). If an event doesn't carry enough information to reconstruct the state, the event schema is incomplete. Adding section_path to payloads is a backward-compatible change (old events without the field default to section_id=0, new events get proper placement). Option B is heuristic-dependent. Option C requires strict ordering. Option D is unacceptable.
payload.tests:
1. Create events with section_path. Fold into fresh DB. Verify invariants.section_id != 0.
2. Run ddis coverage on materialized DB. Verify per-section completeness is computable.
3. Backward compat: fold events without section_path. Verify section_id=0 (graceful degradation).
4. Round-trip: parse spec → emit events → fold → project → parse again → diff = empty.

payload.title: Witness ID stability under upsert
payload.statement: Witness upsert operations must preserve the row ID when updating an existing witness, ensuring FK references from challenge_results remain valid.
payload.semi_formal: forall w in invariant_witnesses: upsert(w) => w.id_after == w.id_before AND forall c in challenge_results: c.witness_id IN invariant_witnesses.id
payload.violation_scenario: InsertWitness uses INSERT OR REPLACE which deletes the old row (destroying its ID) then inserts a new row with a new auto-increment ID. challenge_results.witness_id references the old ID, causing FK constraint failure.
payload.validation_method: Test: insert witness, insert challenge referencing it, upsert witness, verify challenge FK still valid.
payload.why_this_matters: Without ID stability, the witness-challenge adjunction breaks. Challenges become orphaned, Check 17 fails, and the bilateral verification loop is severed.

payload.title: Module relationship materialization completeness
payload.statement: The materialize fold applier must populate the module_relationships table from ModulePayload relationship arrays (maintains, interfaces, implements, adjacent), preserving the module ownership graph for projector filtering.
payload.semi_formal: forall m in ModulePayload: |m.Maintains| + |m.Interfaces| + |m.Implements| + |m.Adjacent| == |INSERT INTO module_relationships WHERE module_id = m.id|
payload.violation_scenario: InsertModule receives ModulePayload with relationship arrays but only stores module_name and domain. The module_relationships table stays empty, breaking projector module filtering and APP-INV-087.
payload.validation_method: Test: emit module_registered event with maintains=[INV-001], materialize, verify module_relationships row exists with rel_type=maintains and target=INV-001.
payload.why_this_matters: Module relationships are the structural backbone of the spec graph. Without them, cascade analysis, implementation order, and projector filtering all degrade to empty results.

payload.title: Refine confidence floating-point fidelity
payload.statement: Confidence dimension scores in the RALPH refinement loop must use floating-point intermediate arithmetic to avoid integer division truncation that distorts dimension selection.
payload.semi_formal: forall d in dimensions: |score_float(d) - score_int(d)| < 0.5 where score_float uses float64 division and score_int uses integer division
payload.violation_scenario: deriveConfidence computes 10 * numerator / denominator using Go integer division. For 1/3, this yields 3 instead of 3.33, causing the wrong dimension to be selected for improvement.
payload.validation_method: Test: deriveConfidence with CompleteElements=1, TotalElements=3; verify coverage score is 3 (rounded), not 3 (truncated from 3.33).
payload.why_this_matters: The RALPH loop selects the weakest dimension for improvement. Integer truncation can make two dimensions appear equally weak when one is clearly weaker, causing the wrong dimension to be targeted.

payload.title: Drift contradiction integration
payload.statement: The drift analysis formula must integrate contradictions detected by the consistency checker, not leave the contradictions counter at zero. The 2x penalty for contradictions in the drift formula must be exercised.
payload.semi_formal: drift.contradictions >= |consistency.Analyze(db, specID).filter(t => t.Type == contradiction)|
payload.violation_scenario: drift.go declares contradictions := 0 but never increments it. The drift formula includes 2*contradictions but the value is always 0, meaning contradictory specs get the same drift score as consistent ones.
payload.validation_method: Test: create spec with contradictory invariants, run drift analysis, verify contradictions > 0 and drift score includes 2x penalty.
payload.why_this_matters: Contradictions are the most severe form of drift — a spec that contradicts itself is worse than one with missing elements. The 2x penalty exists for this reason but is dead code.

payload.title: ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE for witness upsert
payload.problem: INSERT OR REPLACE on witnesses deletes the old row and creates a new one with a different auto-increment ID. This orphans FK references in challenge_results, causing FOREIGN KEY constraint failures.
payload.options: A) ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE (preserves row ID). B) ON DELETE CASCADE on FK (auto-deletes challenges). C) Remove FK constraint entirely (loose coupling).
payload.decision: Use INSERT ... ON CONFLICT(spec_id, invariant_id) DO UPDATE SET ... which preserves the existing row ID while updating all fields. This maintains FK integrity without requiring cascade rules or removing constraints.
payload.consequences: ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE is the correct SQLite pattern for upsert-with-identity-preservation. It avoids the DELETE+INSERT semantics of INSERT OR REPLACE, keeping the row ID stable. CASCADE would silently destroy challenge evidence. Removing the FK would weaken data integrity.
payload.tests: 1. Insert witness (ID=5), insert challenge with witness_id=5, upsert witness, verify challenge.witness_id=5 still valid. 2. Verify LastInsertId returns existing ID on conflict path.

payload.title: Snapshot-accelerated fold CLI integration
payload.problem: The --from-snapshot flag exists on the materialize command but is never read. FoldFrom() exists but is unreachable from CLI. Users cannot benefit from snapshot optimization.
payload.options: A) Wire --from-snapshot to FoldFrom in runMaterializeInternal. B) Remove the flag and snapshots entirely. C) Auto-detect snapshots without flag.
payload.decision: Wire --from-snapshot to FoldFrom. When set, load latest snapshot, verify its state hash, then call FoldFrom with the snapshot position. If verification fails, fall back to full replay (APP-INV-095 graceful degradation).
payload.consequences: Snapshots were designed as an optimization for large event streams. The fold engine and snapshot infrastructure are complete — only the CLI wiring is missing. Auto-detection (option C) is premature without user testing.
payload.tests: 1. Create snapshot at position N, add events, materialize --from-snapshot, verify only events after N are processed. 2. Corrupt snapshot hash, verify graceful fallback to full replay.

payload.title: Witness ID stability under upsert
payload.statement: Witness upsert operations must preserve the row ID when updating an existing witness, ensuring FK references from challenge_results remain valid.
payload.semi_formal: forall w in invariant_witnesses: upsert(w) => w.id_after == w.id_before AND forall c in challenge_results: c.witness_id IN invariant_witnesses.id
payload.violation_scenario: InsertWitness uses INSERT OR REPLACE which deletes the old row (destroying its ID) then inserts a new row with a new auto-increment ID. challenge_results.witness_id references the old ID, causing FK constraint failure.
payload.validation_method: Test: insert witness, insert challenge referencing it, upsert witness, verify challenge FK still valid.
payload.why_this_matters: Without ID stability, the witness-challenge adjunction breaks. Challenges become orphaned, Check 17 fails, and the bilateral verification loop is severed.

payload.title: Module relationship materialization completeness
payload.statement: The materialize fold applier must populate the module_relationships table from ModulePayload relationship arrays (maintains, interfaces, implements, adjacent), preserving the module ownership graph for projector filtering.
payload.semi_formal: forall m in ModulePayload: |m.Maintains| + |m.Interfaces| + |m.Implements| + |m.Adjacent| == |INSERT INTO module_relationships WHERE module_id = m.id|
payload.violation_scenario: InsertModule receives ModulePayload with relationship arrays but only stores module_name and domain. The module_relationships table stays empty, breaking projector module filtering and APP-INV-087.
payload.validation_method: Test: emit module_registered event with maintains=[INV-001], materialize, verify module_relationships row exists with rel_type=maintains and target=INV-001.
payload.why_this_matters: Module relationships are the structural backbone of the spec graph. Without them, cascade analysis, implementation order, and projector filtering all degrade to empty results.

payload.title: Refine confidence floating-point fidelity
payload.statement: Confidence dimension scores in the RALPH refinement loop must use floating-point intermediate arithmetic to avoid integer division truncation that distorts dimension selection.
payload.semi_formal: forall d in dimensions: |score_float(d) - score_int(d)| < 0.5 where score_float uses float64 division and score_int uses integer division
payload.violation_scenario: deriveConfidence computes 10 * numerator / denominator using Go integer division. For 1/3, this yields 3 instead of 3.33, causing the wrong dimension to be selected for improvement.
payload.validation_method: Test: deriveConfidence with CompleteElements=1, TotalElements=3; verify coverage score is 3 (rounded), not 3 (truncated from 3.33).
payload.why_this_matters: The RALPH loop selects the weakest dimension for improvement. Integer truncation can make two dimensions appear equally weak when one is clearly weaker, causing the wrong dimension to be targeted.
